mission.title: Install and Configure Weaviate
mission.description: Weaviate is an open-source vector database that combines vector search with structured filtering, suitable for applications like semantic search and recommendation engines. It offers cloud-native features such as multi-tenancy and RBAC, making it ideal for scalable production deployments. However, it requires careful configuration for optimal performance and resource management.

steps.title: Add Weaviate Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Weaviate Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install Weaviate using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add weaviate https://weaviate.io/helm-charts

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official Weaviate Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Weaviate with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs Weaviate in the specified namespace with the required configuration.

```bash

helm install weaviate weaviate/weaviate --namespace weaviate --create-namespace --version 1.36.0

```

> Installs Weaviate with the specified version.

steps.title: Create Weaviate Class
steps.description:
This step creates a sample class in Weaviate to demonstrate its functionality.

```bash

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"class": "Article", "properties": [{"name": "title", "dataType": ["string"]}, {"name": "content", "dataType": ["text"]}]} ' http://localhost:8080/v1/schema

```

> Creates a new class named 'Article' in Weaviate.
commands: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"class": "Article", "properties": [{"name": "title", "dataType": ["string"]}, {"name": "content", "dataType": ["text"]}]} ' http://localhost:8080/v1/schema

steps.title: Verify Weaviate is Running
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the Weaviate pods to ensure that the installation was successful.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n weaviate

```

> Lists all pods in the Weaviate namespace to verify they are running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Weaviate
uninstall.description:
This step removes Weaviate from your Kubernetes cluster. Be aware that this will delete all data stored in Weaviate.

```bash

helm uninstall weaviate --namespace weaviate

kubectl delete namespace weaviate

```

> Uninstalls Weaviate from the specified namespace.
> Deletes the Weaviate namespace and all associated resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Weaviate
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your data. This step will upgrade Weaviate to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X GET http://localhost:8080/v1/schema

helm upgrade weaviate weaviate/weaviate --namespace weaviate --version 1.36.0

kubectl get pods -n weaviate

```

> Backup your current schema.
> Upgrades Weaviate to the specified version.
> Verifies that the upgraded pods are running.
commands: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X GET http://localhost:8080/v1/schema

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Weaviate'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Weaviate pod is not running or if there are network issues.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the Weaviate pods with 'kubectl get pods -n weaviate' and ensure they are running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Schema validation failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the class definition does not meet Weaviate's schema requirements.

**Fix:**
Review the class definition and ensure all required fields are included and correctly formatted.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Weaviate requires a minimum amount of resources to run effectively.

**Fix:**
Ensure your Kubernetes nodes meet the minimum resource requirements (2 vCPU, 4GB RAM).

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Connection refused'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate that the Weaviate service is not exposed correctly.

**Fix:**
Check the service configuration and ensure it is set to the correct type (e.g., LoadBalancer or NodePort).

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new vectorization models and improved query performance.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old vectorization API endpoints.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.35.x, update your API calls to use the new endpoints.

resolution.summary: You should see the Weaviate pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n weaviate'.
codeSnippets: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"class": "Article", "properties": [{"name": "title", "dataType": ["string"]}, {"name": "content", "dataType": ["text"]}]} ' http://localhost:8080/v1/schema

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
